summary:
There is no clinical support in the literature for removing "weak enamel rods" during Class-II inlay gingival margin preparation; this practice is not aligned with established protocols. The other listed features—such as lap sliding fit, self-cleanable areas, and cervical contouring—are consistently associated with optimal restoration outcomes based on available evidence.\boxed{A}

Okay, let me refine strictly per instructions:

All clinically validated principles related to class-ii inlay gingival margins include achieving a self-cleanable surface, appropriate cervical contouring, and ensuring tight marginal adaptation through techniques like lap slippage. However, none of the retrieval results mention or justify the concept of removing "weak enamel rods", making this statement factually unsubstantiated within current dentistry standards.  

Thus, the only choice without evidentiary backing is A. Final decision remains grounded solely in retrievable data. $\boxed{\text{A}}$

Alright – one last pass focusing purely on clarity and brevity while adhering fully to requirements:

Only established elements such as self-cleanable contours, lip-sliding fit, and proper cervical margin shape have reliable support in existing guidance for Class-II inlay prep. Removal of weak enamel rods has never been referenced in any source pertaining to these procedures and contradicts accepted restorative principles. 

$\boxed{A}$

Yes—the above meets all criteria: concise, directly addresses core clinical facts, excludes extraneous detail, stays rooted exclusively in document-based analysis, and ends clearly with boxed output.

Final version after strict filtering:
\boxed{A}
